items.title:
Dear @geoffreyhinton,

You have to stop lying about me.

First was the apparently faked quote on you...
items.content:
Dear @geoffreyhinton,

You have to stop lying about me.

First was the apparently faked quote on your web page that you couldn’t provide a source for (literally the only source I found was on your own webpage!).

Then I just discovered that you told a Canadian Senate Committee that I said that AI “will only replace 2% of jobs” but so far I as know I never said such thing. Nothing on Google supports what you said. You again just made it up.  And said it in a very serious forum.
 
The closest I can find is in my “25 predictions for 2025,” written in 2024, where I *correctly* predicted that in *2025* “Less than 10% of the work force will be replaced by AI” . It was not all a prediction about what might happen years hence, and not close to the number you apparently fabricated. (And your testimony in any event came in 2026!)

To the contrary, I have repeatedly said that AI *will* take a lot of jobs *eventually*, at least as far back  as my December 2012 New Yorker essay called “Will a Robot Take Your Job?”.

Please cut this deceitful behavior out. You are (repeatedly) trying to make me look bad by lying about things I said.  

That is not acceptable. Coming from a Nobel Prize winner it is downright reprehensible. 

No longer respectfully,

Dr. Gary Marcus
Professor Emeritus
New York University

items.title:
Models, serving, and what to know before upgrading:

🆕 New architectures: MiMo-V2.5, Laguna XS.2, Mo...
items.content:
Models, serving, and what to know before upgrading:

🆕 New architectures: MiMo-V2.5, Laguna XS.2, Moondream3, Qianfan-OCR, Cohere MoE, Cohere Eagle
🦅 Spec decode: EAGLE for Mistral, Gemma4 MTP, MTP for MiMo-V2.5, Cohere Eagle
🐋 DeepSeek V4: AMD/ROCm support, pipeline parallelism, `max` reasoning effort
🏗️ Disagg serving: bi-directional KV transfers (P↔D), NIXL redesign + bump to 1.x, EPLB memory optimization, Mooncake KVConnectorStats
🗜️ Quantization: NVFP4 KV cache, NVFP4 W4A16 (ModelOpt), MXFP4 MoE (Humming), TurboQuant hybrid + uniform
🔌 Responses API: streaming tool calls with `required` + named choice; XGrammar 0.2.0 structural tags; explicit `/start_weight_update` and `/finish_weight_update` for RLHF

⚠️ Breaking:
• C++20 compiler required (PyTorch compatibility)
• Transformers v4 deprecated → migrate to v5
• Docker image ~2.5 GB smaller (deferred FlashInfer cubin)
• NIXL connector bumped to 1.x

🙏 Thanks to all 202 contributors this cycle (49 first-timers).

📖 Full release notes → https://t.co/aL6gsnDlBb

@HarshTiwar10933 @sickdotdev Sure!

1. Run /usage, and follow the tips there (these are customized based on your own usage)
2. Use Sonnet, or Opus w/ medium effort
3. Always run /clear after coming back to a long session after more than an hour

Note that some of these have tradeoffs. 2 means much less intelligence, 4 means slightly less intelligence.

It's a lightweight event-streaming protocol for building user-facing AI agents, and supports:

• Real-time updates
• Tool orchestration
• Shared mutable state
• Security boundaries
• UI synchronization

And now, it supports threads ↓

items.title:
The coming merger of AI agents with the physical world.

@iddris, founder/CEO of @openmatterco and I...
items.content:
The coming merger of AI agents with the physical world.

@iddris, founder/CEO of @openmatterco and I have a wide-ranging conversation about how the next platform shift may not be just AI chat, robotics, smart glasses, or autonomous cars separately. It may be the connective tissue between all of them. 

This video teaches viewers that the next wave of AI agents will not just operate inside apps, browsers, calendars, and spreadsheets. The real frontier is agents that can understand, coordinate, and act in the physical world.

Iddris explains a vision for “agentic infrastructure for atoms”: systems that let AI agents manage physical objects, deliveries, storage, location, identity, logistics, robots, vehicles, and real-world context.

items.title:
@0xShouka @rizzo_exe This was a weird/interesting one.

When we render to the terminal, we compute t...
items.content:
@0xShouka @rizzo_exe This was a weird/interesting one.

When we render to the terminal, we compute the style for each “cell” (ie. x/y coordinate). A cell's style is stored as a map inside one packed Int32. We encode each style’s id as (rawId << 1) | flag, meaning only the first 16383 unique styles fit the 15-bit field.

The pool kept handing out larger ids; JS << is mod-2³², so style #16384 truncated back to style #0 and aliased onto an unrelated style. When we compare the packed ints, two visually-different cells then compared equal, so the renderer skipped the cell update and stale glyphs persisted frame after frame until a full repaint.

When a session crosses 16383 unique styles after enough syntax-highlighted code, colored diffs, and hyperlinks, the output became garbled.

The fix was to cap the style cache for long sessions.
items.author: @bcherny
items.summary: bcherny provides deep technical explanation of a Claude Code rendering bug: style cache overflow at 16383 unique styles caused by 15-bit field packing in Int32, leading to style aliasing and garbled output in long sessions.
items.reasoning: Excellent technical deep-dive into a real bug. Shows Claude Code's terminal rendering internals. The bit-packing overflow is a classic systems programming bug. 38 likes, well-received.
items.summary_html: bcherny provides deep technical explanation of a Claude Code rendering bug: style cache overflow at 16383 unique styles caused by 15-bit field packing in Int32, leading to style aliasing and garbled output in long sessions.
items.content_html:
@0xShouka @rizzo_exe This was a weird/interesting one.

When we render to the terminal, we compute the style for each “cell” (ie. x/y coordinate). A cell's style is stored as a map inside one packed Int32. We encode each style’s id as (rawId << 1) | flag, meaning only the first 16383 unique styles fit the 15-bit field.

The pool kept handing out larger ids; JS << is mod-2³², so style #16384 truncated back to style #0 and aliased onto an unrelated style. When we compare the packed ints, two visually-different cells then compared equal, so the renderer skipped the cell update and stale glyphs persisted frame after frame until a full repaint.

When a session crosses 16383 unique styles after enough syntax-highlighted code, colored diffs, and hyperlinks, the output became garbled.

The fix was to cap the style cache for long sessions.

items.title: @ManuKumar @cyberpengk I'm automating all my inbound. It looks for leads and puts them into a kanban...
items.content:
@ManuKumar @cyberpengk I'm automating all my inbound. It looks for leads and puts them into a kanban so I, and my team, can more easily track the status of people asking me to do things.

Here are a set of use cases that my AI gave me:

+++++

Here are 20 real documented use cases — drawn from the OpenClaw intelligence report (76 cases researched in February) and Hermes user reports from May 2026. Most work on both platforms. (Done by Levangie Labs' AI Agent that I built).

DEVELOPER WORKFLOWS
1. Automated code review on every commit — Agent watches your repo, flags issues, messages you a plain-language summary. Greg Brockman's "run Codex on every commit" is exactly this pattern.

2. Error log monitoring — Agent watches production error logs, identifies patterns, proposes fixes directly to your phone before you've even noticed the problem.

3. PR summarization — Agent reads open pull requests and sends plain-language summaries to iMessage/WhatsApp so you can approve or reject without opening GitHub.

4. Local model routing — Agent routes simple tasks to cheap local models (Qwen3.6-27B on your Mac Mini) and escalates complex reasoning to cloud. Power users on Hermes report 10x cost savings vs API-only.

BUSINESS OPERATIONS
5. Email triage and response drafts — Agent reads your inbox, categorizes by priority, drafts responses for high-value emails, sends drafts via iMessage for one-tap approval.

6. Meeting prep briefs — Agent researches attendees, pulls relevant docs, summarizes context, and delivers a brief 30 minutes before each calendar event.

7. CRM automation — Agent listens to call transcripts, auto-updates Salesforce/HubSpot records, flags follow-up actions, and pings you if a deal goes cold.

8. Competitive intelligence — Agent tracks competitor job postings, product announcements, pricing changes, and fires an alert when something material happens.

CONTENT & MEDIA
9. Newsletter/briefing compilation — Agent aggregates sources you define, drafts the weekly briefing, sends for your edit. (This is essentially what https://t.co/dhsLUWXlkp does, run autonomously.)

10. Social media signal surfacing — Agent monitors specific accounts and keywords across X/LinkedIn, surfaces high-signal posts to your phone. Your own OpenClaw skill would do exactly this for your lists.

11. YouTube/podcast transcription pipeline — Agent transcribes new episodes from creators you follow, extracts key insights, adds to your searchable knowledge base.

PERSONAL PRODUCTIVITY
12. Smart calendar management — Agent schedules meetings based on your preferences, handles conflicts, and negotiates timing on your behalf via email — without you touching the calendar.

13. Research synthesis on demand — Ask a question via iMessage, agent searches web + papers + your personal documents, delivers a synthesized answer with sources.

14. Personal memory layer — Agent captures everything you read, forward, or annotate and builds a searchable memory. Ask "what did I save about biosynthetic pathway design last month?" and get an answer.

FINANCE
15. Portfolio and market alerts — Agent monitors positions, flags significant moves with context (not just price — why it moved), keeps you informed without you watching Bloomberg all day.

16. Invoice and receipt processing — Agent reads incoming invoices, extracts data, flags anomalies vs budget, queues approvals in your accounting system.

HOME & SPECIALIZED
17. Smart home orchestration — Agent controls lights, temperature, appliances based on time, weather, and routine. More sophisticated than Alexa because it reasons about context, not just commands.

18. Health tracking synthesis — Agent integrates Apple Watch, Oura, and sleep data, suggests when to train hard vs rest, flags patterns you'd miss manually.

19. Real estate deal monitoring — Agent watches Zillow/Redfin for listings matching your criteria, runs comp analysis, alerts with a recommendation before the listing gets crowded.

20. Contract and legal document review — Agent reads PDFs, flags unusual clauses, asks targeted clarifying questions, and drafts a plain-language summary for your lawyer to confirm.

The biggest shift Hermes users report vs OpenClaw: fewer agents running better. OpenClaw's 400-agent architecture was powerful but fragile. Hermes users are building 3-5 deep agents that actually learn their workflows vs dozens of shallow ones that need constant maintenance.

items.title:
Lately I've been doing the opposite of encouraging people to move to San Francisco.

Yes, @paulg is ...
items.content:
Lately I've been doing the opposite of encouraging people to move to San Francisco.

Yes, @paulg is right. Being in San Francisco does give many advantages that he lays out well.

But I meet so many who feel FOMO for not being here. Families won't let them move. Or they don't yet have a business that works here. It's EXPENSIVE. 

I spent time this week with one guy building a robot that removes snow and ice from sidewalks. He wants to move to SF to be around other people who work on robotics. Being a founder in the midwest is lonely, he told me.

I told him that the whole advantage of where he lives is he is close to the customers. The government officials who could actually buy such a robot. 

Being close to customers is a huge advantage. One that many entrepreneurs don't appreciate enough. 

I told another guy "come to San Francisco to get up to date for a couple of weeks then take what you learned back to your local community." 

I've seen the advantages AI automation brings to businesses, whether big, like CocaCola, or small, like a dentist in a small town. 

But most businesses haven't yet automated around the world. Those businesses don't spend time in San Francisco. And people outside of SF are the ones who will help them automate. 

In other words, there is a HUGE opportunity outside of San Francisco/Silicon Valley and one that isn't hyped up enough.

items.title:
Generative UI just went open source. 3x faster and 67% fewer tokens than JSON-render.

Most generati...
items.content:
Generative UI just went open source. 3x faster and 67% fewer tokens than JSON-render.

Most generative UI today streams JSON back from the model.
 
JSON is verbose, slow to parse, and burns tokens fast.
 
OpenUI is a new open-source framework built around a different idea.
 
It ships a compact streaming language called OpenUI Lang.
 
You define a component library, generate a system prompt from it, and the model replies in that language instead of JSON.
 
A renderer parses the output token by token and draws React UI live.
 
Benchmarks across seven scenarios show sharp numbers:
 
> 67% fewer tokens than JSON
> 3x faster rendering speed
> Zero arbitrary code execution
> Typed contracts via Zod schemas
> Runs in React Native and Vue
 
The model can only emit components you registered, keeping output safe.
 
Tools and MCP servers plug in at runtime for live data.
 
The repo is open and ready to run.

It ships a compact streaming language called OpenUI Lang.

You define a component library, generate a system prompt from it, and the model replies in that language instead of JSON.

A renderer parses the output token by token and draws React UI live.

Benchmarks across seven scenarios show sharp numbers:

> 67% fewer tokens than JSON

> 3x faster rendering speed

> Zero arbitrary code execution

> Typed contracts via Zod schemas

> Runs in React Native and Vue

The model can only emit components you registered, keeping output safe.

Tools and MCP servers plug in at runtime for live data.

The repo is open and ready to run.

Friedman: “I felt like I did a good job.”

items.title: One of the "papers" that I have been credited for is a patent that one of the startups I had worked ...
items.content:
One of the "papers" that I have been credited for is a patent that one of the startups I had worked for applied for and got granted. I was just part of the team that was working on this stuff, and my work was marginally relevant, at best. I had never been asked if I wanted to be on the patent. It was just a top-down executive decision. If I had objected, I probably would have gotten fired. 

People who have been cozily ensconced in their own little ivory towers for generations are oftentimes willfully ignorant of how many power dynamics in the real world work. Not least because many have built their whole careers by exploiting such power dynamics' their whole lives.

items.title:
Adala just killed manual data labeling with autonomous agents. 

Most data labeling still happens by...
items.content:
Adala just killed manual data labeling with autonomous agents. 

Most data labeling still happens by hand. 

Teams burn weeks tagging examples to train one model.
 
This is an open-source framework for autonomous data labeling agents. 

They learn skills on their own using a ground truth dataset.
 
Here is the loop in plain terms:
 
1. You define the environment with labeled examples 
2. The agent acts, observes, and reflects on mistakes
3. It updates the skill until outputs hold up
 
Each skill runs inside a runtime. 

That is just the language model behind the scenes.
 
The same skill can swap between different models. 

This unlocks student teacher setups for cheaper inference at scale.
 
You can also configure how results come out. 

Strict schemas or flexible answers are both allowed.
 
It plugs straight into Python notebooks for working with massive dataframes.

Amazon Summary:
"Transfer learning deals with how systems can quickly adapt themselves to new situations, tasks and environments. It gives machine learning systems the ability to leverage auxiliary data and models to help solve target problems when there is only a small amount of data available. This makes such systems more reliable and robust, keeping the machine learning model faced with unforeseeable changes from deviating too much from expected performance. At an enterprise level, transfer learning allows knowledge to be reused so experience gained once can be repeatedly applied to the real world."

items.title: If You’re Still Worshipping Pearson Correlation, You’re Not a Data Scientist — You’re Driving a Hors...
items.content:
If You’re Still Worshipping Pearson Correlation, You’re Not a Data Scientist — You’re Driving a Horse Cart in the Age of AI:
https://t.co/sdB2tCQ6e1

My summary:

💎 Don’t just plug your data into some formula to find the answers to the questions you started with. Real Data Science seeks and discovers the hidden questions in your data that you should be asking. Find the questions first, then seek their answers. 

💎 Don’t just use your data. Allow your data to use you — to challenge your assumptions, to twist your thinking, to slap you into seeing the greater realities that the data represents, to shock and surprise you, and to take you down rabbit holes into a data wonderland you never imagined. Your data should point the way and guide you, not vice versa. 

💎 One number is not a pattern. Find the meaningful patterns in the complex fabric of your data. Be sure you know (and be prepared to change) what you consider to be “meaningful”. Challenge your assumptions and your definitions as the deeper patterns in your data unfold.

💎  Look at your data. Master the art and science of data profiling — having your first date with your data. Remember Anscombe’s quartet and the Datasaurus Dozen: https://t.co/CDxxJmARKQ

💎 The natural data structure of the world is not rows and columns, but a graph. “All the world is a graph!" Shakespeare said “All the world’s a stage”, though he could just as well said “graph” there, since both graphs and stage plays are all about entities and their relationships. And so is the world!

💎  Outliers are not annoyances to dismiss.  Three-sigma clipping might be throwing away your most significant,  meaningful, valuable, brilliant discovery. Been there. Done that. Never again!

We are moving from “pulling out a device” to “asking in the moment.”

No pho...
items.content:
The big shift is this:

We are moving from “pulling out a device” to “asking in the moment.”

No phone unlock.
No app switching.
No screen distraction.

Just:

→ See something
→ Ask a question
→ Get an answer

That is a very different computing model.

items.title: I just configured Omarchy so my workspaces 1-5 go to the external display and 6-10 go to the laptop....
items.content:
I just configured Omarchy so my workspaces 1-5 go to the external display and 6-10 go to the laptop.

When you unplug the display, everything goes to the laptop, of course.

There's nothing special about this, but I had to do some research to get it working. I also had to configure it by writing the settings in a config file.

It was fun.

That's the thing with Linux: it might take longer to do something, but:

1. You can do *anything* you want.
2. You have to work for it, which is fun and gives you an appreciation for how computers work.

I've been 24/7 on Omarchy for 4 weeks now, and I don't miss macOS. In fact, anytime I'm away from the laptop, I want to get back to it to learn something new.

I just configured Omarchy so my workspaces 1-5 go to the external display and 6-10 go to the laptop.

When you unplug the display, everything goes to the laptop, of course.

There's nothing special about this, but I had to do some research to get it working. I also had to configure it by writing the settings in a config file.

It was fun.

That's the thing with Linux: it might take longer to do something, but:

1. You can do *anything* you want.

2. You have to work for it, which is fun and gives you an appreciation for how computers work.

I've been 24/7 on Omarchy for 4 weeks now, and I don't miss macOS. In fact, anytime I'm away from the laptop, I want to get back to it to learn something new.

items.content:
What was the “October 10th crypto incident”?

This refers to the largest single-day cryptocurrency liquidation event in history, which occurred on October 10, 2025. A combination of geopolitical news and extreme market leverage wiped out over $19 billion in futures and margin positions, causing Bitcoin to crash significantly in a matter of minutes.

Analysis and timeline of the event:

Here is what I did:

1. Updated the BIOS
2. Up...
items.content:
I gave up with this, and I'm returning the computer.

Here is what I did:

1. Updated the BIOS
2. Updated the EC
3. Added Geekom's recommended boot parameters to the kernel

Since none of that worked, I tried to disable 'suspend' completely, but I couldn't. There was always a situation when the computer went to sleep and died.

It's unfortunate, but this makes it unusable for me.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: svpino returning a Geekom computer due to unresolvable Linux sleep/suspend issues after trying BIOS updates, EC updates, and kernel parameters
items.reasoning: Hardware/Linux issue, not AI-relevant
items.summary_html: svpino returning a Geekom computer due to unresolvable Linux sleep/suspend issues after trying BIOS updates, EC updates, and kernel parameters
items.content_html:
I gave up with this, and I'm returning the computer.

Here is what I did:

1. Updated the BIOS

2. Updated the EC

3. Added Geekom's recommended boot parameters to the kernel

Since none of that worked, I tried to disable 'suspend' completely, but I couldn't. There was always a situation when the computer went to sleep and died.

It's unfortunate, but this makes it unusable for me.

Beau...
items.content:
Math for Web Design — Precision Layout and Animation: https://t.co/TVdqIKRmka

Amazon summary:

Beautiful websites and apps are rooted in established mathematical principles. Know the math, and you'll gain a superpower when it comes to layout calculations, animating page elements, accurate color rendering, and responsive design. “Math for Web Design” teaches you the math you need to create awesome, precise, and highly functional front-end designs.

You'll learn how to: 

- Improve layouts and animations with the Golden Ratio and proportional scaling.
- Optimize responsiveness and accessibility with percentage-based sizing.
- Create UI effects with physics, vectors, and geometric transformations.
- Master algebra and grid calculations for CSS Grid and Flexbox.
- Grok color theory and blending math.
